title: Why July's P&L Is the Most Important One You'll Pull All Year
linkedin:
FY27 started this morning.
Most business owners will review the first month's numbers in September.

That five-week gap between the month ending and anyone reading the result is where the financial year starts to get away from the plan. Quietly, and without anyone deciding to let it happen.

July's P&L is the most useful of the twelve. Not because the numbers are the biggest, but because the cause of any variance is still recent and identifiable, and the year still has eleven months of room to respond.

It shows whether gross margin held from FY26 to FY27. Whether the new award rates are running at the modelled cost. Whether July's revenue is tracking the annual plan. And whether the June 30 debtors are converting to cash at the pace the working capital model expected.

The businesses that consistently hit their annual budgets are not the ones with better plans. They are the ones that compare results to the plan in the same month the results occur.

If gross margin is two points below budget in late July, the cause is still traceable. The award rates went up. The work mix skewed lower-margin in the first few weeks. A pricing change did not take effect on the date assumed. Each of those has a specific response, and that response is available in July in a way it simply is not in September.

There is still time to pull three things before July ends: gross margin to date, revenue against the monthly plan, and cash conversion from June 30 receivables. Three numbers, while the year still has eleven months of room.

Full piece on the ProfitPulse blog. Link in the first comment.

#ProfitabilityAdvice #AustralianBusiness #SMEFinance
facebook:
FY27 started this morning. The numbers from the first month are already building.

Most business owners will look at July's results properly in September, by which point ten weeks of the year have set their own pattern without much active input.

The new award rates are in the payroll. The pipeline is converting at whatever rate it is. The June receivables are clearing, or they are not.

Worth asking: when does your first monthly review actually happen this year, and what does it look at?

We have published a piece on why reviewing July's P&L in July, rather than in September, is one of the few things that genuinely changes a financial year's result.

#AustralianBusiness #SMEFinance
google:
FY27 Has Started: The First Monthly Review Matters More Than It Looks

Today is 1 July, the first day of the 2026-27 financial year. For owner-led businesses across Brisbane, Queensland, New South Wales, and Victoria, the new financial year has already started producing numbers: payroll at the new award rates, revenue against the annual plan, and the June 30 debtors converting to cash at whatever pace they are clearing.

Most businesses will look at July's results properly in September, once the month is well and truly settled. By then, any gap between the plan and the actual result has been running for ten weeks without a response.

July's profit and loss is the most useful one of the year precisely because the causes of any variance are recent and identifiable while the year still has eleven months of room to respond. Whether gross margin held from last year. Whether the award rate increase landed at the modelled cost. Whether revenue is tracking the annual plan in the opening month.

ProfitPulse works with SMEs across the East Coast to build the monthly reporting discipline that makes this kind of early review a reliable rhythm rather than a manual effort.

Read the full piece on the ProfitPulse blog to understand what July's numbers are telling you and what to check before the month ends.
